stem: The most common method of sterilization used in the dental office is:
explanation: Steam autoclaving (using pressurized steam at 250°F/121°C for 15-30 minutes) is the most widely used and recommended method of sterilization in the dental office.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 7

stem: Biological monitoring (spore testing) of sterilizers should be performed at least:
explanation: The CDC recommends biological monitoring (spore testing) at least weekly to verify that the sterilizer is functioning properly. Some states require more frequent testing.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 7

stem: Before instruments are placed in the sterilizer, they must first be:
explanation: Instruments must be thoroughly cleaned (manually or with an ultrasonic cleaner) and dried before sterilization. Debris left on instruments can prevent sterilization from occurring.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 7

stem: Chemical indicators on sterilization packaging indicate that:

options.text: The package was exposed to certain sterilization conditions
The instruments do not need biological monitoring

explanation: Chemical indicators (process indicators) only show that the package was exposed to certain conditions (e.g., heat). They do NOT confirm sterility — only biological indicators (spore tests) verify actual sterilization.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 7

options.text: Using high-frequency sound waves to create cavitation
Circulating chemical disinfectant at high speed

explanation: Ultrasonic cleaners use high-frequency sound waves to create microscopic bubbles (cavitation) that implode and loosen debris from instrument surfaces. This is safer and more effective than hand scrubbing.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 7
